$comment: Curated notes fixture for auto-link precision evaluation. Phase 2 ships ~30 notes across 3 clusters; gate is ≥0.8 precision on the labeled pairs. Grow to 100 notes in a follow-up when real-model CI harness runs.

notes.title: Q2 launch — risks and slips
notes.body: Engineering capacity is tight through July. PRD locked but two integration points (Gmail OAuth + Stripe) are not yet signed off. If CASA review takes the full 8 weeks we miss our committed Plus rollout window. Risk register updated; need to re-baseline next Monday with Mira.

notes.title: Plus rollout — staged release plan
notes.body: Three rings: 5% canary on May 18, 25% on May 25, 100% on June 1. Rollback bar: if brief open-rate drops below 40% in canary, halt. Need analytics wiring per the brief.open RPC route before May 15.

notes.title: Q2 product blockers
notes.body: Top three: (1) Gmail OAuth CASA, (2) embedding model size in extension bundle, (3) cost ledger UI is still a stub. Mira to triage Wednesday standup. Customer council Friday — pre-read by Thursday EOD.

notes.title: Mira 1:1 — May 2
notes.body: Discussed launch slip risk. Mira agreed to push CASA submission this week instead of next. Wants the brief.morning eval gate signed off before launch announcement. Action: I send her the precision numbers by Friday.

notes.title: Pricing memo — three scenarios
notes.body: Plus at $39 versus $49 versus $59 with a 30-day trial. Reviewer feedback: $49 with trial outperforms in modeled retention but risks brand framing. Owner: me. Due Tuesday for the board pre-read.

notes.title: CASA tier-2 readiness checklist
notes.body: Gmail OAuth consent screen frozen. 12-item security questionnaire — drafted 9, three pending on data retention details. Subcontractor list submitted. Expected turnaround: 6-8 weeks. Submitting Friday.

notes.title: Amsterdam trip — itinerary draft
notes.body: Arrive Thursday morning. Rijksmuseum Friday. Day trip to Haarlem Saturday — coastal weather permitting. Sunday slow morning before flight. Booked a small flat in De Pijp. Need to pre-book Anne Frank tickets — they sell out 6 weeks ahead.

notes.title: Packing list — light Europe trip
notes.body: Convertible jacket, two pairs jeans, three button-downs, sneakers, dress shoes. Adapter (Type C). Kindle. Notebook + pen. Backup charger. No checked bag — carry-on only since we're hopping flats.

notes.title: Anne Frank house — booking note
notes.body: Tickets release 6 weeks before, in two waves. Both released at midnight CET. Watched March windows go in 12 minutes. Set a calendar alarm for the May release date. Backup: get-in-by-line option for 30 minutes pre-open.

notes.title: Restaurants — pre-pick list
notes.body: De Kas (greenhouse-grown). Foer (small natural wine). The Pancake Bakery (touristy but the kids will love it). Coffee at Lot Sixty One. Skip Vondelpark cafe — slow service per the food forum.

notes.title: Haarlem day trip — what to see
notes.body: 30 minutes by train. Frans Hals museum. Grote Markt for lunch. Beach at Bloemendaal if the weather holds. Last train back is 23:14 — plenty of room. Pack a windbreaker — coastal can shift.

notes.title: 10k training — week 5 plan
notes.body: Monday: 5km easy. Tuesday: 6x800m at 5k pace. Thursday: tempo 4km at threshold. Saturday: long run 12km. Sunday: cross-train (cycling). Strength work Tuesday + Friday evening. Heart rate cap 162 on easy days.

notes.title: Form cues — cadence drills
notes.body: Target cadence 178. Forefoot strike. Light ankle, push off the toes. Exhale on the off-foot to break breath sync with stride. Drilled 4x100m yesterday — felt smoother after the third rep.

notes.title: Race day — taper plan
notes.body: Week 8 starts the taper: cut total volume 30%, keep intensity. Two days rest before race. Hydrate carefully Friday + Saturday. Race kit laid out Friday night. Strategy: even splits first 5km, push from km 7.

notes.title: Tempo run notes — May 6
notes.body: Felt strong through km 3, started to break form at km 3.5 — cadence dropped to 174, leaned forward. Last km cleaned up after a deliberate cadence reset. Recovery 71 next morning, mid-range.

notes.title: Strength work — accessory routine
notes.body: Glute bridges 3x12, single-leg deadlift 3x10 each side, calf raises 3x15, plank 3x45s. Twice a week. Skip if a hard run is within 24 hours. Focus on slow eccentric for the deadlifts — that's where the resilience builds.

notes.title: Power and Progress — ch 4 notes
notes.body: Acemoglu and Johnson argue that productivity gains rarely diffuse to wages without countervailing institutional pressure. The 19th-century textile industry analogue is the throughline. Useful framing for current AI labor displacement debates.

notes.title: Power and Progress — ch 7 reflections
notes.body: The Bell Labs vs. modern Silicon Valley R&D contrast is the most generative chapter. Bell socialized basic research with weak appropriation; Silicon Valley structurally appropriates everything at startup formation. Different equilibria, different downstream effects on diffusion.

notes.title: Industrial revolution diffusion — what stuck
notes.body: Productivity divergence held for almost a century before wages began to track. The lag closes only after organized labor + suffrage. The book's most uncomfortable claim: without those countervailing forces, the lag persists indefinitely.

notes.title: Compare with Brynjolfsson on AI productivity
notes.body: Brynjolfsson is more optimistic about AI diffusion timelines. Acemoglu would say Brynjolfsson under-weights the structural appropriation. Read 'The Productivity J-Curve' next — that's the empirical anchor for the optimistic view.

notes.title: Note to self — book club discussion prep
notes.body: Lead question for Tuesday: which contemporary technology best fits the Acemoglu countervailing-pressure framework? My answer: open-source LLMs, but the framing weakens because the 'countervailing' actor is itself a corporation (Meta). Need a sharper example.

notes.title: Power and Progress — ch 9 quote
notes.body: Direction of technological change is a choice, not a destiny — paraphrasing the closing argument. This is the book's actionable thesis. The mechanisms section (ch 10) gets vague though; the policy prescriptions feel under-specified.

notes.title: May retro — what went well
notes.body: Phase 1.5 settings landed clean with multi-provider keys. e2e gate caught the encryption-onboarding regression before merge. Pre-commit hooks + commitlint kept commit history readable. Daily-agent slice closed in under two weeks.

notes.title: May retro — what hurt
notes.body: Sqlite-vec / sqlite-wasm incompatibility surfaced mid-phase 2 semantic-notes and forced a pivot to JS cosine. Should have validated the vec stack end-to-end during Phase 1 instead of just scaffolding. Loss: ~3 hours of replanning + the orphan implementer commit.

notes.title: Subagent-driven dev — observations
notes.body: Worked great for mechanical tasks (migrations, repo methods, type extensions). Less great for tightly coupled refactors — overhead of dispatching + reviewing each one didn't amortize. Doing tasks 5-9 as one cohesive inline write was the right call once the JS-cosine pivot landed.

notes.title: Test infra debt — bench harness
notes.body: Vitest bench is fine but the 10k-note corpus generation is slow. Want a pre-built sqlite snapshot committed under tests/perf/fixtures/. Saves ~30s per bench run. Tradeoff: snapshot needs re-generation when schema changes.

notes.title: Embedding model swap — design surface
notes.body: Schema v3 has notes.embedding_model + notes.reembed_pending columns. v4 migration will flip the flag on stale-model rows. Background job processes them in batches. Need to handle the case where a model swap happens mid-batch — version-check on each chunk.
